question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: cleanup()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a race condition in the cleanup() method—where FileSystemUtils.deleteRecursively(tmpDir) runs concurrently with another request writing EVM state to the same tmpDir—to corrupt in-flight contract execution state, causing a fund-transferring transaction to silently fail or misroute value?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: cleanup()] Can an unprivileged external user pre-create a symlink at the path resolved by System.getProperty('java.io.tmpdir', '/tmp/web3') before application startup, causing FileSystemUtils.deleteRecursively(tmpDir) to traverse and delete critical filesystem paths that the EVM executor depends on for fund-related state, leading to loss of funds during contract execution?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: cleanup()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that cleanup() silently swallows IOException and continues application startup with a corrupted or missing tmpDir, causing the EVM execution layer to write contract state to an unexpected location and produce incorrect fund transfer results?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: cleanup()] Can an unprivileged external user manipulate the java.io.tmpdir JVM system property via a crafted request that triggers property injection, causing cleanup() to resolve a different tmpDir path and delete files needed for correct EVM fund accounting?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user supply crafted args[] to SpringApplication.run(Web3Application.class, args) through an exposed management endpoint or environment variable injection, causing the application to load a malicious configuration that redirects fund-related contract call results?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger application restart via an exposed Spring Boot actuator endpoint, causing cleanup() to delete tmpDir mid-execution of a fund-transferring contract call, resulting in loss of the in-progress transaction state?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: cleanup()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the TOCTOU (time-of-check-time-of-use) window between FileSystemUtils.deleteRecursively(tmpDir) and Files.createDirectories(tmpDir) to inject a malicious directory or file that the EVM executor later reads as trusted state, causing incorrect fund transfer outcomes?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: cleanup()] Can an unprivileged external user cause cleanup() to fail silently by filling the filesystem (disk exhaustion), preventing Files.createDirectories(tmpDir) from succeeding, so that subsequent EVM contract executions involving value transfers fail in an unhandled way that misreports success to the caller?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: cleanup()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that cleanup() uses System.getProperty('java.io.tmpdir', '/tmp/web3') without sanitizing the path, allowing path traversal if the property contains '../' sequences, causing deletion of EVM state directories that store fund-related contract data?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the @SpringBootApplication auto-configuration loaded by Web3Application to trigger loading of a malicious auto-configured bean that intercepts ContractExecutionService.processCall() and manipulates the value field of fund-transferring calls?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the @Import(CommonConfiguration.class) import in Web3Application to override a bean definition in CommonConfiguration that controls fund-related account resolution, causing getSenderAccountID() to return the wrong payer account for value transfers?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: cleanup()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a path injection in the java.io.tmpdir property to cause cleanup() to delete the directory used by the EVM's writable cache state (ContractCallContext.getWriteCacheState), causing fund-related state writes to be lost and balances to be incorrectly reported?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: cleanup()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that cleanup() does not validate that tmpDir is within an expected base path, allowing a crafted java.io.tmpdir value to point to a shared directory used by multiple application instances, causing cross-instance state corruption that affects fund transfer results?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Spring Boot application context loaded by SpringApplication.run() to inject a crafted HTTP request that bypasses ThrottleManager.throttle() validation, allowing unlimited contract calls with value transfers that drain gas buckets and cause legitimate fund-transferring calls to be rejected?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the application startup sequence in main() to send requests before the ThrottleManager bean is fully initialized, bypassing gas throttling and submitting high-value contract calls that exhaust the gas bucket, causing denial of service for legitimate fund-transferring users?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Spring Boot application context refresh triggered by SpringApplication.run() to cause a bean initialization ordering issue where TransactionExecutionService is used before AccountReadableKVState is fully populated, resulting in fund-transferring calls resolving to the wrong sender account?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Application.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: cleanup()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that cleanup() does not check if tmpDir is a regular directory before calling FileSystemUtils.deleteRecursively(), allowing a pre-placed device file or named pipe at that path to cause the application to hang or corrupt EVM state used for fund accounting?
